Platform overview

Logbook · rota · assessments · AI · community

Scaling timeline

Full AKU departmental coverage

AKUTracker is a web-based training management app built for the MMed Obstetrics & Gynaecology residency programme at Aga Khan University Hospital Nairobi. It replaces paper logbooks with a structured digital platform covering procedural competency, work-based assessments, AI-assisted exam preparation, duty rota management, and community learning. Any new workflow — forms, checklists, sign-off processes — can be designed and built into the platform. Every new feature goes through a design review process: a visual mockup is shared with the requesting department, iterated to satisfaction, and only then implemented.

Scaling roadmap · click any node to expand

How it works
Access · privacy · devices

How the tracker works

The tracker is a web app that runs in any browser on any device — phone, tablet, or laptop. No installation is required. It can also be saved to the home screen like a regular app and works even with a weak internet connection.

All data is stored securely in the cloud. Every resident sees only their own records. Supervisors see records submitted to them for sign-off. Programme Directors see their entire cohort. Nobody outside your programme can access your data.

Who can access
AKU email required · approved by PD
Works offline
Logs saved · syncs when reconnected
Any device
Phone · tablet · laptop
Data security
Encrypted · each user sees only their data
Always up to date
Updates deploy automatically
Feature design process
Mockup → review → iterate → build

Who can access the app

Access requires an official AKU email address. New users register and are approved by the Programme Director before gaining full access.

Works even without internet

The tracker works in low-connectivity environments. If a resident has no signal in theatre or on the ward, the app still loads and allows data entry. Everything syncs automatically the next time the device connects.

Any device

Works on any modern phone, tablet, or laptop without installing from an app store. On Android and iPhone it can be added to the home screen for one-tap access, opening full-screen like a native app.

Data security & privacy

All data is encrypted in transit and at rest. Every user can only see their own records. Supervisors see only what has been submitted to them. Programme Directors see aggregated cohort data without accessing individual clinical notes.

A note on developer access: The developer also holds a resident role within the programme. Technical administrative access is required for system maintenance — equivalent to an IT system administrator. This access is logged and available for audit. It does not provide access to other residents' clinical records through the app.

Always up to date

The tracker updates itself automatically. When an improvement is deployed, every user sees it the next time they open the app — no action required on their part.

How new features are designed

Any department requesting a new workflow goes through a structured design process. A visual mockup is produced and shared with the requesting department, revised as many times as needed, and only once the department is satisfied is the feature built.

Current features · v2.2
Live and in use today

What is live right now

All core modules are in active use by OBGYN residents and faculty at AKU Nairobi.

Procedure logbook
58 procedures · colour-coded progress
Learning log
Events · learning needs · overdue alerts
Duty rota
Auto-generated · live hours tracking
Assessments & sign-off
OSATS · ISASF · export · CBL rota
AI exam practice
MRCOG questions · shared question bank
Community & messaging
Shared wisdom · messages · anonymous feedback

Procedure logbook

Residents log each procedure with date, role, supervising doctor, and notes. Progress is shown as a colour-coded traffic light: red (<40%), amber (40–79%), green (≥80%).

Calendar learning log

A monthly calendar where residents record learning entries tagged as an Event, a Learning Need, or an Action. Learning needs that have not been addressed trigger an overdue warning. A quick floating button is available on every screen for rapid entry.

Duty rota

An interactive schedule generator. Rules are entered once and the system produces a compliant rota automatically. A live hours dashboard shows each resident's running total with warnings when limits are approached. An AI assistant answers rota queries in plain language.

Assessments, sign-off & CBL

OSATS and ISASF completed digitally: the supervisor fills in the form, rates each domain, and signs off with a secure digital signature. Completed forms are instantly available and exportable as PDF. The CBL rota manages teaching session schedules with interactive pre-tests and horizontal and vertical feedback flows.

AI exam practice

Every session generates a fresh MRCOG-style exam question. All questions are saved to a shared question bank accessible to every resident. Faculty can add their own questions to the bank.

Community, messaging & feedback

The Shared Wisdom feed lets residents and faculty post clinical tips and learning points. Files and documents can be attached and shared within the app. Short in-app messages allow direct communication. Anonymous feedback can be submitted at any time.

Roles & access levels
Who sees what

Who sees what

Access in the tracker is strictly role-based. Each person logs in and sees only what is appropriate to their role.

On developer access: The developer — who is also a resident in the programme — requires administrative system access to maintain and update the platform. This is equivalent to an IT system administrator. All administrative actions are logged and available for audit by programme leadership at any time.

Resident (PGY1–4)
Own records only · no peer visibility
Faculty / Supervisor
See assessments submitted to them
Programme Director
Cohort overview · no private notes
Admin & system
Platform maintenance · access logged

Resident

A resident sees only their own logbook, assessments, learning log, and rota shifts. They cannot view another resident's records under any circumstances.

Faculty / Supervisor

A supervisor can see assessment forms submitted to them for sign-off and procedure log entries that reference them. They cannot browse the general logbook of any resident.

Programme Director

The PD sees a cohort-level dashboard: colour-coded progress across all residents, pending assessments, dissertation milestones, and rota compliance. The PD does not have access to free-text notes in residents' personal log entries.

Admin & system access

The admin role is used to maintain the platform. All administrative actions are logged and available for audit. This is a practical necessity for any hosted software system — comparable to a hospital IT administrator having server-level access to an electronic patient record system.

Phase 0 · Foundation for growth
Behind-the-scenes work · 4–6 weeks

Phase 0: Preparing for multiple departments

This phase happens entirely behind the scenes. Current OBGYN users will notice no change. The work reshapes the app internally so that a second department can be added without rebuilding anything from scratch — like converting a single-family house into a block of flats.

Department separation
Each dept has its own space & data
Flexible procedure lists
Each dept sets its own procedures
Custom features per dept
Each dept sees only what it needs
Institutional admin panel
Manage all departments from one place

Department separation

Each department becomes a fully isolated space. A Surgery resident cannot see an OBGYN resident's data. This is enforced at the database level — not just a display filter.

Flexible procedure lists

Each department uploads or enters their own list of required procedures with their own target numbers and categories. No technical work is needed each time — a department coordinator manages this through the admin panel.

Custom features per department

Not every department needs every feature. A department without a rota simply will not see a rota tab. Features are configured once during onboarding and can be updated at any time.

Institutional admin panel

A central admin view allows oversight of all departments: which are active, how many users each has, which features are enabled, and pending registration requests across the institution.

Phase 1 · Second department
General Surgery pilot · 4–6 weeks

Phase 1: Onboarding General Surgery

General Surgery is the natural first expansion: it shares the same training framework as OBGYN, already uses OSATS (which is built), and has a well-defined procedure list. Success: a Surgery resident logs in, sees a Surgery logbook, completes a Surgery OSATS, and gets a Surgery exam question — with no trace of OBGYN content.

Surgery procedure list
Uploaded by Surgery coordinator
Mini-CEX & DOPS forms
Additional Surgery assessment tools
Surgery exam questions
AI generates Surgery-specific MCQs
Surgery rota rules
Configured to Surgery call patterns

Surgery procedure list

Adding Surgery's procedures requires no technical work. A coordinator enters the list and the logbook for Surgery residents displays it automatically with colour-coded progress tracking.

Mini-CEX and DOPS

General Surgery uses Mini-CEX (a brief clinical encounter observation) and DOPS (Direct Observation of Procedural Skills) in addition to OSATS. Both are added as new digital forms with the same supervisor sign-off workflow.

Surgery exam questions

The AI question generator accepts a clinical specialty as input. For Surgery residents it generates Surgery questions instead of OBGYN questions — same format, different content. This requires only a configuration setting, no code change.

Surgery rota rules

Rota rules are configured per department during onboarding: shift types, maximum hours per week, pairing requirements. Once configured, the rota generator applies these rules automatically each month.

Phase 2 · Medical specialties
Internal Medicine + Paediatrics · 6–8 weeks

Phase 2: Expanding to medicine

Internal Medicine and Paediatrics log clinical encounters rather than surgical procedures. Phase 2 introduces an encounter-based logbook alongside new assessment tools used in these departments.

Case discussion form (CBD)
Structured case review with sign-off
Clinical encounters logbook
Diagnosis-based · for Internal Medicine
Age-based tracking (Paeds)
Neonatal vs paediatric competencies
Universal form engine
All assessment types from one system

Case-Based Discussion (CBD)

CBD is a teaching and assessment tool where a resident presents a case they managed and the supervisor discusses their clinical reasoning. The digital CBD form captures structured ratings and free-text feedback with a two-party sign-off.

Clinical encounters logbook

For Internal Medicine, the logbook tracks clinical encounters categorised by diagnosis group — acute chest pain, sepsis, diabetic emergencies, and so on. The same colour-coded progress tracking applies.

Age-based competency tracking

In Paediatrics, many skills are age-specific. The logbook tracks progress separately across age bands (newborn, infant, child, adolescent) for procedures where this distinction matters.

Universal form engine

A single flexible form engine reads the structure of any assessment type and displays the correct fields, rating scales, and sign-off sections. Adding a new assessment type requires only configuring a new template, not writing new software.

Phase 3 · Anaesthesia + Nursing
Simulation log · portfolio export · 6–8 weeks

Phase 3: Simulation and portfolios

Anaesthesia training relies heavily on simulation. This phase introduces a simulation log module. Nursing introduces tracking competencies across multiple clinical rotations. Both benefit from a complete digital portfolio export.

Simulation log
Scenario · assessor · debrief · rating
Rotation tracking
Multiple placements in one training year
Portfolio export
Complete signed PDF · ready to submit
Regulatory reporting
KMPDC · other supervisory bodies

Simulation log

Anaesthesia residents log each simulation session: date, scenario, learning objectives, assessor, global rating, and debrief notes. Simulation records count towards the portfolio alongside real patient encounters.

Rotation tracking

Nursing students move through several clinical placements within a single training year. The tracker allows a user to have a current active rotation, with all log entries tagged to that placement. The portfolio view aggregates across all rotations.

Portfolio export

A complete portfolio exported as a PDF: procedure logbook with progress summary, signed assessments, learning log entries, exam question performance, and rota hours. The export carries a digital verification mark confirming it has not been altered since export.

Regulatory reporting

The Kenya Medical Practitioners and Dentists Council (KMPDC) requires verifiable training records for licensing. The portfolio export is designed to satisfy this directly with digitally signed, time-stamped records. Other supervisory bodies can easily be provided with a version tailored to their specific data requirements.

Phase 4 · Programme oversight
PD dashboard · institutional reporting · 8–10 weeks

Phase 4: Giving leadership a real-time view

Phase 4 builds dashboards that show training quality and workload trends in real time — without needing to ask residents or collect data manually.

Programme Director dashboard
Whole cohort at a glance
Cross-department comparison
Anonymous benchmarking across specialties
Automated reminders
Pending assessments · milestones · approvals
Institutional overview
All departments · engagement · trends

Programme Director dashboard

One-screen summary of the entire cohort: which residents are on track, falling behind, or at risk across all procedure categories; pending assessments; rota compliance; dissertation milestone status. Answer "How is my cohort doing?" in under a minute.

Cross-department comparison

Anonymous aggregate data enables comparisons: time to competency, assessment completion rates, cohort trends. Shown only in anonymised form to Programme Directors and the Academic Office.

Automated reminders

The platform sends reminders at key moments: new user approval requests, signed OSATS notifications, overdue assessment sign-off reminders, and dissertation milestone alerts — sent both within the app and by email.

Institutional overview

A senior administrator or Academic Office representative can see a summary across all departments: total active users, most-used modules, assessment completion trends, and which departments may need support.

Phase 5 · Beyond AKU Nairobi
AKU Health Services network · East Africa · South Asia

Phase 5: The wider AKU network and beyond

Aga Khan Health Services operates hospitals across East Africa, South Asia, and Central Asia. The platform is designed from the outset to support multiple institutions. Each institution would have its own version with its own domain, admin hierarchy, and departments — while sharing the same underlying system.

AKHS network
Nairobi · Dar es Salaam · Kampala · Karachi
Learning system integration
Connect with AKU’s e-learning platform
Research output
Training data · publishable findings
Other institutions
Any African medical school can adopt it

AKU Health Services network

AKHS hospitals in Dar es Salaam, Kampala, Kisumu, Karachi, and other cities run training programmes. Each institution can have its own version of the platform while the core software is maintained in one place.

Integration with AKU’s learning systems

If AKU operates a broader e-learning platform, the tracker can be connected so training records and assessment data do not need to be entered twice. The tracker becomes the clinical competency layer of AKU's educational infrastructure.

Research output

With multiple departments logging training data over several years, the tracker generates a dataset AKU does not currently have: longitudinal records of competency acquisition, working hours vs learning outcomes, and AI-assisted exam preparation efficacy. The dataset grows automatically with every procedure logged.

Other institutions

The platform can be adopted by any medical school or teaching hospital. A new institution needs its own internet domain, a small cloud database subscription, and a coordinator to configure their departments. No specialist IT infrastructure required.

Full AKU departmental coverage
28–38 weeks · phased rollout · design-first approach

Bars show relative effort, not calendar dates. Phases run sequentially. Adding a second developer compresses the timeline proportionally.

Research outputs

Study 1
How quickly do residents learn?
Time to reach competency across training years and departments. Target: Academic Medicine.
Study 2
Does overwork affect learning?
Working hours correlated with assessment scores. Policy-relevant for KMPDC.
Study 3
Does AI practice improve exam results?
AI question engagement vs MRCOG Part 1/2 outcomes. Before-and-after design.
Study 4
Digital vs paper: is the data reliable?
Comparing digital logs against supervisor-verified paper records.
